speaker: interviewer
text: Six. I'm interviewing Paul Oppenheimer in Sunninghill, England. Paul, you went to school in Berlin, in London, and in Holland. How did the schools compare with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that I'm in any position to compare the schools. I just went to them. And I did as I was told. And I enjoyed them, really. I mean, this 'did as I was told' features a lot in my story. And my parents, you see, especially my father, I think, was brought up as a good German. And he always did as he was told, as we find out. And he believed that, as long as you'd do as you were told, did as you were told, everything would be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were of the same mold. And on the whole I believe most people were like that. I am amazed at the way people behave nowadays and our children behave. In those days, we just all always did as we were told.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that there was a certain Germanic thing behind it as well. But even in Germany, I think it's probably changed. But certainly, in Britain, everything has always been very much freer where everybody can do what they like and not take any notice of the rules and regulations. And especially in later years, I've found that this idea of just sticking to the rules was not really the way that people lived in Britain kind of thing. But maybe there's lots of interesting corollaries when it comes to European directives, which I worked with later in my life. Surprisingly, it's Britain who's always doing exactly everything as you were supposed to do it, according to the European directives. Whereas the Germans and the French very often take no notice of it. But, anyway, that's beside the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were we? School. Recently-- a comparison. I wouldn't like to compare the stringency or the severity of the school curriculum. I think I'm in no position. I certainly wasn't in any position to do so at that age there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only find out now that I never stayed at any school very long. And I never stayed in any country long enough to learn the complete syllabus properly. I've never really learned English properly. I mean, I did six months in 1936, so I learned almost nothing about the grammar of England. And when I came to England after the war, I-- I specialized, really, in the technical subjects. I did have to learn some English because, obviously, I [? had to ?] write everything and speak everything in English. So I did learn it. But again, I picked it all up. And I've never really learned the-- the grammar. And in Germany, where I went to school for first two or three years, maybe, of my life, until the age of seven, I don't suppose I learned much grammar there. So I've never seemed to have learned the grammar of any language. It's all been picked up. And a similar sort of thing happens with history, for example. The-- In Germany, I don't think I learned history. I was too young. But in Holland, I learned history. And I only found out very much later in life that the English history, which I never learned, is very different from the Dutch history, because the Dutch tended to fight the English very often. They were both Maritime nations with big empires to protect. And they had big-- big navies. And I learned everything from the Dutch side. And they were always winning. But I found out that the English claimed that they won most of the battles. And the Dutch have their admirals, Piet Hein, and people like that, and never heard of Nelson, anything. So it's interesting in a way, really, that-- that I've never really learned British history. And people are always on about all these kings and queens of the past. And I've never really had any of that in my life. So maybe those are things that I've missed in my life, but I managed to do without. But it is interesting that-- I think I've added it all up-- that I went to eight or 10 different schools in-- in a 10-year period, from 1935 to '45, and in three different countries, three different languages. And, yeah, it must have sort of, been a bit difficult to acclimatize each time and to learn. But I seem to have managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't think so. No. In any of them, no. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where were we? I think we were still in Naarden, and at the stage where they were beginning to introduce all those laws, the registration cards, first of all, the 'J', the [INAUDIBLE] Israel, to the names. And then, where we got the yellow star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still have the original yellow star, one of my original yellow stars, because we all had several of them. And I still have mine with Jude on them. I proudly show it to school children, kind of thing. It's a tangible thing that went right through the war with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have memories of wearing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we always had to wear it kind of thing on our outer clothing. And we usually had it on pullovers like this, because you could wear the pullover with lots of different clothes, rather than have to have it on each shirt, or whatever you were wearing. So pullovers were a convenient way of wearing the star. And had on overcoats that we wore, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: The interesting thing about it, which I found out later on, really, because many of these things I don't really remember, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an interesting item is how I remember all this long. I gave a talk recently to a school in Hampshire with Stephen Smith, actually. And when we came to the question period, there was a young boy of about 12. And he said, sir, how do you remember all this? [LAUGHS] And I was a bit taken aback by the question, kind of thing. But I managed to answer it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, basically, there are some things which I shall always remember, like the 10th of May, 1940, and even the 23rd of August, when-- when these two boys had their birthdays, and that we lived in Berlin, and that we lived in Tempelhof, and that there was an airport there. And there are lots of things that I shall always remember forever. And then, there are lots of things that I've remembered by talking to my brother, Richie. We-- we stimulate each other. We say there was this. And then he said, oh yeah, I remember that. And then there was that. And somehow, between us, when we've done taped interviews between ourselves for [INAUDIBLE] kind of thing, we stimulate each other into remembering things. Memory is a funny thing. I'd not realized. Because of the fact, when I say, I-- I never remember this thing. If Richie says to me, you remember. On Friday night, we had the service. And mother lit the candles. It's-- I don't remember that. But very often, with things like that, it comes back into my memory then. There are loads of things in my memory, which I just can't recall until somebody mentions it. And when somebody mentions it, then I can remember it quite clearly. So that was the second way of recalling things. The third way was that I've got all these documents. I don't remember that I ever went to a Jewish school in Berlin. But I've got a report to prove it here, so I must have done. And I have loads of other photographs of the olden days, and the days in Holland, and all that sort of thing. And they have obviously stimulated me to remembering lots of things that I'd forgotten before. And that was the third way. And the fourth way is by reading books and talking to other survivors. And when I meet other survivors, they remind me of certain things that they experienced. And then I say, oh yeah. I remember that. We had that. And, you know, it-- it isn't that we're trying to make our story better or enlarge upon our story. It's just that I've forgotten certain things, until, again, another survivor says, you remember, in Westerbork, we had showers and all this. And I'd forgotten that we had showers. Well, that wasn't a typical thing, but something like that. They would say, we had this in Westerbork. And then, I can remember it quite clearly again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those were four different ways, which I [INAUDIBLE]. I was very grateful for that question came in on how I was able to remember it. So now, when we go to the yellow stars, I don't remember it. But I've now found out from reading the books that we actually had to pay money. We had to buy the yellow stars. And what was even worse, we had to give up clothing coupons.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, in Holland already, everything was rationed. Food and clothing was rationed. And we had to give up, I think it was a quarter clothing coupon per yellow star. So it wasn't the matter that we were given the yellow stars. We-- we had to wear them, and we had to buy them. And we had to give up precious clothing coupons as well, according to the literature. Obviously, I don't remember that. But we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was, in some ways maybe, one of the worst things. Although, we didn't really mind walking around with a star in Holland. The Dutch people hated the Germans, because they'd occupied their country. We were in a very different situation than Germany, or Poland, or any of those kind of countries. In Holland, all the Dutch people hated the Germans, and I think because they'd overrun their country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were also feeling the pinch kind of thing. They didn't have to wear stars, but they also had a ration book, food rationing and clothing rationing, because the Germans were just shipping everything off to Germany. So they were on our side, really. And there were occasions when all the Dutch people would wear the star, like a Denmark kind of thing, and when the Dutch people all went on strike in support of the Jews. And, generally, they helped us and supported us whenever they could. And there was nothing wrong with going around wearing the star. You didn't have to be ashamed of it or hide it kind of thing. You could almost be proud of wearing the star. It wasn't really a big bother. The trouble with it is-- was that they could pick out, straightaway, who was a Jew. And of course, then, we had more and more restrictions. We were not allowed to go to swimming pools. And we were not allowed to play sports. And those kind of things, as far as I'm concerned, was really bad. And there, of course, you couldn't do it, because you now had the star. And then they could see straightaway. You know [INAUDIBLE] you couldn't go into a football team and play with your star. And without the star, it wasn't allowed. Because in public, you had to wear the star. So we couldn't play football anymore. We couldn't go to the swimming pools. You just weren't allowed in when you went there with your star. Couldn't go in. And the many shops, we were not allowed to go into the shops. And then we were not allowed to go to the cinemas, not allowed to go to concert, not allowed in the theaters. And we were not allowed to do anything. And then someplace in The Hague, you're not allowed to sit on the park benches. It was a bit like apartheid, really, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, later, we were not allowed to go on public transport. And very much later still, we had to give up our bicycles or any other transport we had. But the only thing we had was bicycles. And we had to even hand in our bicycles, our last means of transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, we'd moved to Amsterdam, because there was another rule, that all Jews had to move to Amsterdam. All Jews in Holland had to live in Amsterdam, which, of course, was part of the overall plan, so that they could get us all together and pick us up easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to move from Naarden. And I think it was early in 1942 we moved to Amsterdam, like all Jews had to by that time, because many of them, or most of them, probably, lived in Amsterdam to start with. Did you take all your possessions with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we did, yes. Yes. [INAUDIBLE] We never knew whether we took all our possessions from Berlin. But we took a lot, because we know that we had both an upright piano and a grand piano. And, in fact, the grand piano is in Naarden in this fortress. We-- Before we went into the camps kind of thing, we gave all those things for safekeeping to lots of people. And this grand piano, it was too big to go into the flat that we went to in Amsterdam. So we left it in Naarden, and we gave it to the church. And after the war, we found that it was still in the church. And they asked whether we wanted it back. [LAUGHTER] But we hadn't really got room for it, to take it with us to London [INAUDIBLE]. My uncle had a grand piano already anyway. So we left it in-- in Naarden. And as far as I know, it's still in Naarden now, kind of thing. I don't suppose many people realize that it was ours, or my father's really. So he was musical. And-- and-- and they, and my uncle as well, they were very much into opera, and Wagner in particular, which is not the right sort of thing, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And those are the other things that we missed, kind of thing. We never had any artistic kind of education or [INAUDIBLE] maybe. I mean, we never learned about music, so I'm useless at music. We never had any. For-- for-- for about 10 years, I suppose, we never really heard music, because later on, we were not allowed to have radios, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything was only what we could do ourselves. And we'd given up our pianos, and so we didn't do any of it. But there was another thing that I'd really forgotten about, until quite recently, was that we had these pianos and that my father played them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved to Amsterdam. And in Amsterdam, there is a Jewish area, not far from the central station, really, where most of the Dutch Jews lived for centuries, really. But we went to live in the southern part of Amsterdam. It's called the Rivers Area, [DUTCH]. The Rivers Area, where all the straits were named after Dutch rivers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to live in Vechtstraat-- Vecht was a river somewhere in Holland-- in one of these apartment. They have these long apartment blocks in the streets with three stories above each other, a bit like townhouses, but all kind of joined together. It's a typical Dutch invention, I think. And all the films that you see of Anne Frank and all that sort of thing, you keep seeing these streets with those sort of houses. And we lived in one of those. And, actually, Anne Frank lived just around the corner in Merwedeplein. Another good word to say in English, Merwedeplein. And lots of the German refugees-- because the Franks came from Frankfurt in 1933, so they came a lot earlier than us. And they went straight to Amsterdam. And they lived there. And Mr. Frank, of course, had their business, et cetera. And we probably went to the same school as Anne Frank, because they lived around the corner. And by that time, we had to go to school in Jewish schools. And Anne Frank was one year younger than me, two years older than Richie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember meeting her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We don't remember. She wasn't famous in those days, I tell everybody kind of thing. So we may well have met, but we don't remember it. No. I think she played mainly with girls. And we played mainly with boys in those days. So, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was Amsterdam. And we lived in Vechtstraat 163, second floor, kind of thing. And now-- it was-- there were lots of Jews living in that area, mainly the German refugees. What I hadn't realized-- again, until more recently-- that there was a certain division between the real Dutch Jews that always lived there and the Jews who had come from Germany, mainly, and Austria, kind of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The refugee Jews all came to Holland in the '30s. And there was a certain amount of friction, I understand, although I never encountered any of it. But we didn't live in the same area, really. We lived in south. And the old Dutch Jews lived in their area where they'd lived for centuries before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the fiction all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I hear now. And you'll find later on in the story. You'll find that there are situations where the friction did occur, kind of thing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your-- was your father working at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- obviously, by that time, that was another one of the rules, that Jews were not allowed to live in-- work in banks. And, presumably, this was a Jewish bank. I should imagine it was taken over by Dutch people, or by the Nazis, or whatever kind of thing, and closed down. So he didn't work there. I'm not sure that he did any kind of work. But he was later on taken on by the Judenrat, which was the Jewish council. Now, this was something that comes throughout the story. The Germans were very clever in administering all these racial laws. They obviously had the experience in Germany of how to do it. And, basically, they got the Jews to administer all the laws themselves. The Germans would say what the laws were, and then the Jews had to make sure that they were carried out. And anybody who trespassed kind of thing, first of all, was dealt with by the Jews. And this is where the beginnings of the friction occurred, because in many places, the German Jews got themselves into the best positions kind of thing. Not necessarily Amsterdam, but later on, in Westerbork, it was like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Amsterdam, the Germans set up the Judenrat. And in fact, there was two Dutchman, a man called, Asha, who I think, believe, was a diamond man with loads of money, and a man called Cohen, who I think was a professor or something like that. They were in charge of the Judenrat, the Jewish administration in Amsterdam. And they got themselves a team of people to help them. And this team grew, and grew, and grew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, I found out eventually, there were two-and-a-half-thousand people, Jews, working for this Judenrat, administering all the various rules and regulations. And my father, I found out, also worked for the Judenrat. And the big benefit of that, of working for the Judenrat, was that you were exempt from deportation. Because in 1942, they, with all the other rules and regulations that we had, we weren't allowed to do this, that, and we weren't allowed to do anything, more or less. There was a curfew [INAUDIBLE]. I forgot to mention that. We were not allowed out after 8 o'clock in the evening-- [INAUDIBLE] before-- between 8 o'clock in the evening, 6 o'clock in the morning, we were not allowed outside our houses. And all those kinds of things they could enforce very easily, because we had the star and the identity card with a J. And if you walked around without the star, you were liable to be instantly sent to a concentration camp or various punishments. You-- you were threatened, if you did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about concentration camps at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. But we were always threatened with being sent to-- to the East or sent away to camps kind of thing. I'm not sure whether they were described as concentration camps. When-- when we heard that term first, I can't really remember. But it sounded bad, anyway. It was a bad thing. And we were basically, again, sticking to the rules. You know, my father wouldn't do anything that wasn't according to the rules. So if you had to wear the star, we wore the star. I mean, we-- an interesting thing was that, by that time, when we were in Amsterdam and my father worked for the Judenrat, my father then recognized the benefit of Eve being British. I don't know whether he recognized it earlier where this was always planned, but we found out on our list of documents-- well, we haven't actually found the actual documents, but we have this handwritten list of documents, which maybe we gave to somebody and we never got back. But those listed all this correspondence that my father had with the Red Cross and with the Swiss embassy. Because at that time, there was no British embassy in Holland anymore, because they were at war, Holland being under Germany was at war with Britain. So there was no British embassy in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all communications with British embassy was done via the Swiss embassy in Holland, in Hague. And they had to go to the Swiss embassy in Switzerland, and from there, to the British embassy in Switzerland, and from the British embassy in Switzerland to Britain, when there was any questions kind of thing. And there we got certification that Eve was truly British. And we got a letter, apparently, from them. We haven't got the letter, but we did. And the Germans accepted that, that she was British, because, as I said, later on-- later on, I found that there was a difference between having-- we didn't have a British passport. Eve didn't have a passport. I mean, why would a baby or a six-month-old in Britain, why would she have applied for a passport? Maybe she should have done, but she didn't. So she didn't have a British passport, but we did have a birth certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, my father, in 1942, got this confirmation by the British embassy in Switzerland, and the Red Cross, and the Swiss embassy, et cetera, that she was a British subject. And that entitled her to numerous exemptions. First of all, she didn't have to wear a star. Children under six didn't have to wear a star anyway. But Eve was just about on the border of six and seven in those days. She didn't have to wear the star. And she was not subject to various other rules. And at that time, already, we were told that she was liable-- she was-- she had the potential of being exchanged against Germans. And this is the first time that we heard about that. Whether my father knew about all this before, I don't know. But we were told that in a letter from the German officials, that Eve and her family might be exchanged against Germans which were interned in Britain. That was a big plan, which we didn't know about at that time. But, again, I found out after the war that there was a big plan whereby the German foreign office, von Ribbentrop, who was the chief, he wanted to get all Germans back from wherever they were. And he wanted to-- he didn't want to give up prisoners of war, British prisoners of war, because they would be fighting against them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German plan was that they would give any other Britons and their relatives that they had within their grasp kind of thing-- and obviously, one that they would be keen to give up would be Jews and their family, which were of no use to the Germans anyway. And if they could get Germans back, preferably German prisoners of war--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they also, we hear, demanded two Germans for each Jew. Or sometimes, the story was five and even 10 Germans. So this is why, perhaps, we never got exchanged. But that was the plan which this chap, von Ribbentrop, in the foreign office had. And apparently, there was a big battle between him and Himmler, who was the chief of the Gestapo and the SS. He wanted to kill all the Jews. And he didn't want to give them up. And the story goes that Hitler, in the end, decided that a limited number of Jews should be kept for exchange. Something like 10,000 Jews could be kept for exchange purposes. And that is basically what apparently saved us. So we knew about the exchange, we didn't know all the details of the plans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you did know at that time that you might be used for exchange?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we got this-- my father got this organized, and when he was in Amsterdam in 1942. According to this list of documents, we had letters from the Germans saying that Eve and her family might be exchanged against Germans in Great Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw these letters yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember seeing the letters. But I can show you the handwritten documents where it says that was-- was in-- that was the gist of the letter that we had, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, for example, the fact that Eve didn't have to wear the star was quite interesting. Richie remembers these king of things. We were not allowed to shop. That was another thing. We were only allowed to shop in Jewish shops. And Jewish shops never had anything, because there was food rationing and clothes rationing. So you can imagine that Jewish shops didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the other shops, in ordinary shops, we were only allowed in between 3:00 and 5:00, I think, something like that. And by that time of the day, everything was sold out, if it was any good, usually. So it was very difficult to get any food and clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Richie says that we used Eve, because she didn't have to wear a star. She was allowed to go into the ordinary shops and buy things. But she was only about six or seven years old, so we had to tell her exactly what to get, and give her the money kind of thing, and then send her into the shop. So we were standing outside with our stars. We'd send her in. And then she'd come back out again and say, what did you say what I had to get? [LAUGHTER] How many do I have to get of this? And apparently, you know, everybody was watching, kind of thing, [LAUGHS] while we were manipulating Eve to buy things for us in these shops. Can you remember doing this with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No. But Richie told it. Actually, I'd be interested whether Eve remembers. I shouldn't think so. But it sounds all right. Richie, of course, was born to this whole idea. He was a-- scavenger, is one of the words, but an entrepreneur is another word, kind of thing. You know, he was ideally suited to the camps and all the life that goes with it. And he had all these ideas. He and my mother, apparently, was good at it. Well, they apparently went out very often outside Amsterdam, which were not allowed to do. But they took the star off, Richie says, unbeknownst to my father, who wouldn't have liked it. They took their star off and went outside Amsterdam to the farms and all that sort of thing and bartered for food with jewelry, or any other kind of things that we had. There was another law whereby-- there was a Jewish bank, called Lippmann, Rosenthal, in Holland. It was a big bank, Jewish bank, which was taken over by the Nazis. And all Jews had to hand over all their possessions, basically, to that bank. And the monies, shares, jewelry, everything, we had to hand over to this bank, Lippmann, Rosenthal kind of thing. And we never saw it again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were many of these laws in there, we had, going through my notes kind of thing. I can't remember them all, but other people will remember. So we're now in 1942 when they started deporting people from Amsterdam. First of all, it was done on a voluntary basis. People were invited to volunteer to work for the Germans in camps. And no doubt, there were certain incentives whereby they would be treated well. And they would be fed and everything else, if they volunteered.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, obviously, not many people volunteered. So then it became compulsory. And first of all, they had lists. So the Judenrat had to produce lists. The Germans would say, this week we want 5,000 Jews. And the Judenrat then had the very difficult task of deciding which 5,000 Jews that lived in Amsterdam had to be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever discuss this at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In fact, I didn't know he worked for the Judenrat. But, again, Richie remembers it. And also, we have this list of all these documents where we have a letter to say that he was an inspector working for the Judenrat. We believe that he worked in the financial department. He helped the Jews to fill in the forms for handing over all their stuff to Lippmann, Rosenthal. Lippmann, Rosenthal was a bank. And he was a bank man, so he understood how to fill in the forms and whereby they had to transfer all their shares and any other kind of documents that they had to Lippmann, Rosenthal. So we believe he helped them with doing that job. That was what he did at the Judenrat.
